citation: Al-Fraihat, D., Joy, M., Masa'deh, R., & Sinclair, J. (2020). Evaluating e-learning systems success: An empirical study. Computers in Human Behavior, 102, 67-86.
index_status: Scopus Q1 (Computers in Human Behavior, Elsevier). Verified via OpenAlex: W2968091587. Cited by 1,240.

D1_TECH.evidence: Framework includes System Quality (reliability, functionality, response time, ease of use) and Technical Infrastructure quality as explicit dimensions. Operationalized through survey items validated empirically.

D2_PED.evidence: Information/Content Quality is included (accuracy, relevance, completeness). However, content quality is generic IS quality — no domain-specific operationalization for language learning, SLA principles, feedback theory, or test alignment.

D3_INST.evidence: Includes Service Quality and Support (instructor responsiveness, technical support). However, does not cover learning analytics dashboards, data interoperability standards (LTI/xAPI), or administrative decision-support specifically.

D4_HighStakes.evidence: No coverage of high-stakes test specificity, IELTS/TOEFL rubric alignment, CEFR descriptors, washback, micro-learning, or spaced repetition. Framework is generic e-learning quality.

D6_MultiStakeholder.evidence: Framework considers both learner and instructor perspectives in its empirical study design. Administrator perspective is partially addressed via Institutional Support dimension but not fully operationalized.

D5_Rigor_separate.evidence: Provides validated survey items (CFA confirmed) for most dimensions. However, some items rely on self-report perception rather than objective platform measurement criteria.

notes: Highest-scoring IS framework in corpus. Strong technical coverage but lacks language domain specificity entirely. Serves as the best existing IS-domain evaluation model for general e-learning.
